study of the Sacro Donzetti case, so Hupka invited him to come and talk to the class about it, so he made a study of the Sacro Donzetti case, and I'm sure this will be a very interesting talk.
I wish I could stay to hear it.
And then there was, in that same four years, we were all hired sight unseen.
Anyway, Mr. Childs came with a good officer.
We were at some gathering, he discovered that Frank Pierce was the speech teacher.
All the rest of the evening, Mr. Childs went around, teach his speech, doesn't know Robinson.
His speech, doesn't know Robinson.
Frank went to him at the end of the, he said, Mr. Childs, he said, tell me who this Robinson is.
He said, somebody I should know.
Did any student or anyone ever tease you about being from Illinois, not being local, not being a Texan?
Oh yes, oh yeah, I got that.
I got called a Yankee every day.
I remember one early teacher evaluation.
I don't know actually what the student meant, he said he wants to impress his Yankee ideas.
Did, did everyone ever tease you?
Did they ever think, indicate they thought you were liberal or conservative or anything like that?
Yeah, we had a few essays, you know, here on liberal issues.
I think they could see that I endorsed them more or less.
Let's see, any, any sad, poignant stories, recollections, experiences?
Did the department handle that?
Everybody grieved, of course.
Did they ever contact their families, or flowers for the funeral?
The things you do was rather devastating to us.
The other young man was killed on Labor Day at Planners.
They had to convince a number of students.
It was a big shock for them.
As a matter of fact, I come to think about it, I didn't.
Those students didn't come to talk to me.
I did what I could to try to get the man to let me.
Now, were you and the women in your life, were you all Presbyterians early on here?
Here we were, I grew up a Baptist.
Baptist.
And when we went to, went to my first teaching job, we went from town, which was no Baptist church, probably wouldn't have sampled it had there been.
So I went with Margaret to the Presbyterian and fell in love with the church.
When you moved here, which church did you join?
We went to, we joined in Pride.
It was much closer.
You see, we were at the field.
Yeah.
And we liked it very much.
What, in the, in the late 40s, early 50s, what was the relationship between Bryan and College Station?
When you all first moved here, there wasn't a good grocery store in College Station.
Where'd you all do your shopping?
There actually were a couple of stores.
There was Charlie's in the north over there, called the Knife Depot Travel Agency.
And a Mike Smith had a grocery store there.
What was there right here to the north and west toward the railroad on Jersey?
Was there, weren't there some, was Maidly's Pharmacy there yet?
Yeah, Maidly's Pharmacy.
For those that don't remember Maidly's, describe that.
Well, it was a good old fashioned drug store.
They had all the stuff.
It was sort of a, everybody knew Mr. Maidly.
To hang out for the kids.
It was quite an institution, which a lot of us still miss.
What else was there along there?
I believe Brewers was.
No alcohol in Brazos County then?
Went across Whiskey Bridge.
And that went way back, didn't it?
Did you and your family have any interaction with the students living in the project houses?
Well, the project houses, they were on their way out, I believe, if I recall correctly.
But we did not have.
Okay, let's see, we talked about coaches.

Okay, let's, as we're really getting close to the end, let's make believe that someone walks up to you now and gives you a check worth at least hundreds of thousands of dollars, if not more than that.
And the only restriction to that gift is you must pass it on to Texas A&M.
But you can decide the use or uses.
Use or uses to which that money would be put.
What would you give, if you had lots of money to give away to A&M, what would you give it for?
What are your pet projects?
It's a strange thing to be done with salad, but it's certainly not bricks and mortar.
Is there any special subject area in the library?
I want to do something for the school center.
I certainly want to direct all of it where it benefits people.
I suppose what I actually would do is find out where the needs are and what some of those areas actually want and actually need.

All right, let me conclude then by thanking you very much and by reminding you if you have any papers, personal papers, reminiscences or any sort about the A&M years that you'd be willing for the archives to copy, please do so.
Okay.
Because we need as many actual personal items as possible.
Well, actually I pretty well read that stuff.
Okay.
See, what I wish more than anything else would be the letters that students wrote home.
Because there they were usually very confidential and very honest about what was happening and who was helping them.
I had to vacate my office.
I gave most of them to them.
I gave the Folsom and Twain to my daughter.
Well, let me thank you very much.
Thank you.
